caveats: a: Occupation evidence is for all paper manufacturing, not this six-digit industry.
a: No source measures task-level AI exposure or existing AI use in sanitary-paper plants.
rho: This is bounded judgment, not a measured adoption forecast.
rho: FDA evidence applies to menstrual products within the broader industry, not tissue and towel lines.
e: Provided n relies on size-class receipts and an industry margin bridge rather than observed EBITDA.
e: The industry spans household tissue, away-from-home products, diapers, and regulated menstrual products with different economics.
s5: No complete six-digit control-transfer numerator and eligible-firm denominator were found.
s5: Internal recapitalizations and asset-only plant sales must be excluded.
q: No source directly measures sharing of AI-derived savings.
q: Capture differs materially by branded, private-label, institutional, and commodity channels.
d5: No complete constant-price, constant-quality five-year forecast was found.
d5: The product mix includes mature tissue and potentially faster-growing absorbent-hygiene uses.
o: FDA oversight evidence applies only to menstrual products, though it demonstrates operator accountability within that segment.
o: Import displacement can remove domestic-operator demand even when US consumption persists.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring unit sales through retail/private-label programs, distributor replenishment, and institutional supply arrangements, typically governed by purchase orders, price lists, bids, or supply contracts.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, non-operating product brands, pure wholesalers, upstream paper mills, non-paper reusable hygiene products, non-control financing vehicles, and operations that cannot transfer independently of the owner.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market converters of facial tissue, toilet tissue, towels, napkins, disposable diapers, sanitary napkins, tampons, and related sanitary paper products that repeatedly supply distributors, institutions, retailers, hospitality, foodservice, healthcare, or private-label customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation and employment structure for the broader NAICS 322 paper-manufacturing subsector rather than wage-weighted AI task exposure in NAICS 322291. It reports 253,800 production and nonsupervisory employees out of 356,500 total employees in June 2026 and 87,050 paper-goods machine operators in 2025. I use this physical-production dominance to constrain exposure, while allowing higher exposure in planning, customer administration, documentation, procurement, forecasting, and back-office work. Highly automated sanitary-product lines may have fewer direct operators but more technical and compliance roles, so the direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for all paper manufacturing, not this six-digit industry.
No source measures task-level AI exposure or existing AI use in sanitary-paper plants.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-time study from independent 322291 plants, covering direct production, engineering, quality, planning, commercial, and administrative roles and documenting current automation, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: Core converting, line changeover, materials movement, sanitation, maintenance, packaging, laboratory work, and physical quality control cannot be substituted by language-model software. AI can reduce labor in forecasting, order handling, production planning, specification comparison, deviation documentation, procurement, and customer support.

caveats: No complete constant-price, constant-quality five-year forecast was found.
The product mix includes mature tissue and potentially faster-growing absorbent-hygiene uses.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted Census product shipments and physical-unit data for tissue, towel, diaper, napkin, menstrual, and incontinence categories, combined with major distributor and retailer scanner volumes, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Toilet tissue, towels, napkins, diapers, and menstrual products serve recurring hygiene needs with limited digital substitution. Population, aging, healthcare, and away-from-home activity support quantity, while reusable alternatives, waste reduction, dispenser efficiency, demographic shifts, and customer down-gauging limit growth.

caveats: Provided n relies on size-class receipts and an industry margin bridge rather than observed EBITDA.
The industry spans household tissue, away-from-home products, diapers, and regulated menstrual products with different economics.
e.change_evidence: A verified list of the 45 dataset-estimated LMM firms with ownership, standalone financials, customer recurrence, regulatory registrations, and normalized EBITDA would replace this estimate.
e.rationale: Most independent converters repeatedly supply external customers, but the dataset-estimated economic band can include captive divisions, plants inside large strategic groups, firms whose normalized margin differs from the industry bridge, and commodity operations without durable transferable customer relationships.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 322291 ($2,310,215,281) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 322291 ($14,060,983,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $753,738 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 322291); class <5: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,906,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,011,766 -> IN $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,757,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $550,417 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,491,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $804,432 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,983,545 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,220,589 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $124,528,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,243,469 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $472,268,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $69,187,262 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 45

caveats: FDA oversight evidence applies only to menstrual products, though it demonstrates operator accountability within that segment.
Import displacement can remove domestic-operator demand even when US consumption persists.
o.change_evidence: Customer substitution studies, share data for reusable products, import penetration, vertical-integration plans, and interviews with institutional buyers and private-label customers would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Remaining demand is for physical absorbent and hygiene products, so accountable conversion, testing, release, packaging, and fulfillment remain necessary. Software may automate commercial and documentation steps but cannot wholly self-serve the physical product; reusable alternatives and vertical integration mainly reduce demand or shift supplier ownership.

caveats: No source directly measures sharing of AI-derived savings.
Capture differs materially by branded, private-label, institutional, and commodity channels.
q.change_evidence: Representative supply contracts, bid calendars, customer concentration, price-change clauses, open-book provisions, renewal outcomes, and gross-margin bridges after productivity investments would establish actual retention.
q.rationale: Unit-priced products allow temporary retention of workflow savings, but private-label customers, distributors, institutional bids, retail negotiations, and large branded competitors create strong repricing and pass-through pressure. Regulated or specification-heavy products may retain more benefit through reliability and lower error costs than commodity tissue and towels.

caveats: This is bounded judgment, not a measured adoption forecast.
FDA evidence applies to menstrual products within the broader industry, not tissue and towel lines.
rho.change_evidence: Before/after plant deployments with sustained labor-hour savings, error and deviation rates, model-validation records, and workflow adoption across at least 10 independent firms would materially update the estimate.
rho.rationale: Forecasting, order administration, document review, planning assistance, and quality-record workflows are implementable within five years, but legacy manufacturing systems, validation requirements, customer specifications, data fragmentation, cybersecurity, and the need for accountable human release constrain realized deployment.

caveats: No complete six-digit control-transfer numerator and eligible-firm denominator were found.
Internal recapitalizations and asset-only plant sales must be excluded.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-matched ten-year transaction set from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or Axial, paired with owner-age and succession-plan interviews for the eligible candidate universe, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: A mature manufacturing base should generate retirement, strategic, and sponsor-led control transfers, but capital intensity, concentration among large incumbents, family succession, and buyer caution around commodity or regulated plants reduce the fraction of eligible firms that close a qualifying sale in any five-year window.

principal_driver: Durable replenishment demand and document-heavy planning, quality, and customer workflows support recurring operational improvement.
principal_weakness: The directly AI-substitutable wage pool is small because most value creation is physical and large buyers can force productivity gains into price.

sources.supports: BLS reports 356,500 total employees and 253,800 production and nonsupervisory employees in June 2026, plus 87,050 paper-goods machine operators in 2025, for the broader paper-manufacturing subsector.
sources.title: Paper Manufacturing: NAICS 322

sources.supports: BLS defines the occupation as setting up, operating, or tending machines that convert, saw, corrugate, band, wrap, box, stitch, form, or seal paper or paperboard and reports 96,460 workers nationally in May 2023.
sources.title: Paper Goods Machine Setters, Operators, and Tenders

sources.supports: Census defines the industry as converting purchased sanitary paper stock or wadding into facial tissues, handkerchiefs, table napkins, toilet paper, towels, disposable diapers, sanitary napkins, and tampons.
sources.title: NAICS 322291 Sanitary Paper Product Manufacturing

sources.supports: FDA states that all menstrual products marketed in the United States are regulated as medical devices and must meet applicable manufacturing, labeling, malfunction-reporting, and adverse-event requirements; certain products such as tampons undergo FDA review.
sources.title: Menstrual Product Options, Facts, and Safe Use

sources.supports: FDA states that cleared tampons are single-use medical devices and that manufacturers submit testing on material safety, absorbency, strength, integrity, and bacterial-growth effects before legal sale.
sources.title: The Facts on Tampons—and How to Use Them Safely

title: Sanitary Paper Product Manufacturing
